Editors and hiring teams

How to price video editing work

Pricing gets clearer when both sides define the real job: deliverables, footage volume, revision rounds, deadline pressure, and creative ownership.

Start with deliverables

A single 30-second cut, a weekly YouTube episode, a podcast package, and a paid social variant set should not be priced the same way. Name the finished assets first.

Account for hidden work

Ingesting footage, syncing audio, organizing assets, finding music, captioning, motion graphics, exports, review management, and versioning all add labor beyond the visible edit.

Tie revisions to the agreement

Editors should define included revision rounds. Hiring teams should explain who gives notes and how quickly approvals happen. Revision ambiguity is one of the fastest ways for a fair rate to become a bad deal.

Need an editor?

Send a hiring brief and start the early matching queue.
